CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized (Intro -> Method -> Experiments -> Conclusion) and the main contributions are explicitly listed early in the Introduction. However, the notation and methodological description are sometimes vague or inconsistent (e.g., the element-wise power function and its indexing in Equations 2–3, unclear timing of pseudo-class updates in Section 2.1/2.2, and the gradient update for γ in Equation 5). Figures (e.g. Figures 4 and 5) are informative for intuition, but the presentation is messy (Table 1 is referenced but not shown and figure formatting/numbers are hard to parse). The writing has repetitions, occasional grammatical errors and typos (e.g., “stars”→“starts”, plural/singular mismatches) that reduce clarity and would benefit from careful copy-editing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a specific combination: an EM-style training rule that matches MLP output vectors to a target distribution (Sec. 2.1) and a paired/co-training architecture of two parallel MLPs that use each other’s pseudo-classes as targets (Sec. 2.2). The authors explicitly relate their idea to prior work (Sutskever et al.’s ODM and Siamese networks) and highlight differences, so the contribution is clearly differentiated but builds on known mechanisms (distribution-matching, pseudo-labeling/EM, and pairwise supervision). Overall this reads as a non-trivial but incremental synthesis rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so moderate novelty is appropriate.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper demonstrates a feasible training procedure and provides experimental comparisons (Sections 3–4) showing better association/purity than K‑means/HAC and a reasonable gap to the supervised MLP, so the main empirical claim is supported. However the mathematical description is incomplete: it never specifies the network output activation/normalization, omits the derivative ∇_γ cost and how the power(zi,γ) gradient is computed/implemented or why this constitutes an EM guarantee (Section 2.1), so the theoretical treatment is underspecified. The experimental methodology has reasonable baselines (supervised MLP and two clustering methods) and multiple folds, but lacks variance/significance reporting and ablations (sensitivity to number of pseudo‑classes, φ, batch size) and assumes known class count/uniform φ (acknowledged in Conclusion). Overall the contribution is plausible but has notable gaps in derivations and missing analyses, so I rate it a 3.
